description:
A developer has asked you (the AI assistant) to turn the current working directory into a neat “package-and-release” workflow that demonstrates very basic process management (backgrounding, PID capture, waiting, and logging).  
    The system already contains a small toy project located at  

        /home/user/project  

    with the following structure and contents:  

        /home/user/project/README.md              “Demo project for packaging”
        /home/user/project/src/main.py            print("Hello, packaged world!")

    The developer wants four things to happen, in order:  

    1. **Create a compressed archive** of the entire project directory *in the background* and capture the PID of that compression process in a file.  
       • Archive name **must be**: release-v1.tar.gz  
       • PID file **must be**: /home/user/archive.pid  

    2. **Wait** for that specific background process to finish and record its exit status.  
       • The exit status must be captured exactly as the numeric value returned by the wait command (so “0” on success).

    3. **Move** the completed archive into a new directory called /home/user/releases/ (create the directory if it does not exist).

    4. **Generate a single verification log** at /home/user/logs/release.log using the exact four-line format shown below.  The automated grader will read this file verbatim, so spacing, colons and line breaks matter.  Angle brackets (< >) indicate variable text you must substitute; everything else is literal:

            ARCHIVE_NAME:release-v1.tar.gz
            PID:<numeric-pid-from-archive.pid>
            EXIT_STATUS:<exit-status-from-wait>
            MOVED_TO:/home/user/releases/release-v1.tar.gz

       • The PID line must contain the same PID that is written to /home/user/archive.pid.  
       • The EXIT_STATUS line must contain “0” if the archive finished without error.

    Acceptable command count: roughly 3-4 distinct shell commands (though you may compose pipelines as needed).

    When you are finished, two files and one directory must exist:  

        /home/user/archive.pid                  (single line PID)  
        /home/user/releases/release-v1.tar.gz   (the archive)  
        /home/user/logs/release.log             (the four-line log)

    The autograder will verify:  
    • release-v1.tar.gz is a valid gzip archive whose top-level folder is “project” and whose internal files exactly match the originals.  
    • The PID in archive.pid is numeric and matches the PID recorded in release.log.  
    • EXIT_STATUS is “0”.  
    • The MOVED_TO path is exactly “/home/user/releases/release-v1.tar.gz”.  

    Perform the whole workflow from the terminal.  Do not hard-code any PID; gather it programmatically at run time.
truth:
Initial file system before the agent starts:

    /home/user/project/
        ├── README.md
        │       Contents (one line, no trailing spaces):
        │       Demo project for packaging
        └── src/
                └── main.py
                        Contents (one line, no trailing spaces):
                        print("Hello, packaged world!")

    Expectations after successful completion:

    1. /home/user/archive.pid  
       • Contains exactly one line, the PID of the background tar (e.g. “12345” plus a trailing newline).

    2. /home/user/releases/release-v1.tar.gz  
       • Valid gzip; when extracted it yields a single top-level directory named project containing README.md and src/main.py with byte-for-byte identical contents to the originals.

    3. /home/user/logs/release.log  
       • Exactly four lines terminated by newline characters:  
            ARCHIVE_NAME:release-v1.tar.gz  
            PID:<same PID as /home/user/archive.pid>  
            EXIT_STATUS:0  
            MOVED_TO:/home/user/releases/release-v1.tar.gz  

    The grader will validate that the tar archive exists, the log file exists with the precise format, and that no process with the stored PID is still running once the script finishes.
